Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → Object modeling and domain relationships

LLD learning path

Object modeling and domain relationships

Practice identifying entities, value objects, aggregates, and collaborations so your designs stay cohesive as requirements grow.

Module progress 0%

0 of 3 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Entities, value objects, and aggregates

Use identity, immutability, and transactional boundaries to decide how domain concepts should be modeled.

Why this module matters Design responsibilities cleanly before patterns start to stack up.

Move through the lessons in order so each object-model decision has context before follow-up requirements and machine-coding trade-offs appear.

Study target 3 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

Low-level design interview prep 3 lessons 0/3 complete Follow in order for best results Back to LLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the prompt shape, object boundaries, and extension seams before coding anything.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
LLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Separate identity-bearing entities from immutable values
  • Choose composition, association, or inheritance based on behavior
  • Keep state and workflows aligned with domain boundaries

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core object or workflow decision in your own words.
  • Sketch the key classes, responsibilities, and extension seams before you code.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions, invariants, and object boundaries.
  • Justified abstraction choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of follow-ups such as testing, concurrency, and extensibility.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/3 complete

Entities, value objects, and aggregates

Use identity, immutability, and transactional boundaries to decide how domain concepts should be modeled.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 2 Open
Composition, inheritance, and polymorphism

Use shared behavior carefully so the design stays extensible without coupling unrelated workflows together.

20-30 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Workflow orchestration and state modeling

Separate durable state from coordination logic so long-running workflows remain understandable and extensible.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Entities, value objects, and aggregates

Open

Use identity, immutability, and transactional boundaries to decide how domain concepts should be modeled.

Why it matters

Many LLD prompts become simpler when you distinguish long-lived entities from descriptive values and group consistency rules under the right aggregate root.

What you will cover

Find the objects with identityPromote descriptive data into value objectsChoose aggregate boundaries

Good interview signal

  • Identify which concepts need stable identity.
  • Use value objects for validated, immutable data.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Composition, inheritance, and polymorphism

Open

Use shared behavior carefully so the design stays extensible without coupling unrelated workflows together.

Why it matters

Low-level interviews often probe whether you can reuse behavior without locking future requirements into a brittle inheritance tree.

What you will cover

Prefer composition for optional behaviorUse inheritance for real substitutabilityPresent the trade-off clearly

Good interview signal

  • Use composition when behavior varies independently.
  • Use inheritance only for genuine substitutability.
20-30 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Workflow orchestration and state modeling

Open

Separate durable state from coordination logic so long-running workflows remain understandable and extensible.

Why it matters

LLD prompts often expand from a single method to a multi-step workflow. Good designs keep orchestration explicit instead of burying it across many collaborating objects.

What you will cover

Model the command flowKeep domain state authoritativePrepare for follow-up requirements

Good interview signal

  • Identify the entry point for each major workflow.
  • Keep state transitions owned by domain objects.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
